title: Guided Practice: Preparation, Application, and Removal
narrationScript: You now know the front half of the penetrant process. Preparation, lighting, application, dwell, and removal. This lesson puts the pieces together the way a working shift does, and trains you to spot the exact step where a process went wrong. Walk the line with me twice. First job, a machined steel valve body in the field, visible dye, solvent technique. Before anything else, check the light, because if you cannot see, nothing else matters. White light at the inspection surface, commonly at least one hundred footcandles, measured with a meter, not guessed. Preparation next. The part carries light machine oil, so wipe it down with solvent until a fresh cloth comes away clean, and give the solvent time to flash off so the flaws are dry, not just the surface. Confirm the part sits inside the standard temperature band, roughly forty to one hundred twenty-five degrees Fahrenheit. Now spray the red penetrant over the test area and a band around it, start the timer, and keep the film wet for the full dwell, commonly at least ten minutes. Removal is two wipes. A dry lint-free cloth takes off the bulk. A solvent-dampened cloth takes off the rest. Never flood the part with solvent. The part is now ready for developer, which we cover in the next module. Second job, a rack of aluminum castings on a fluorescent water-washable line. Preparation is an alkaline wash to cut the heavier soils, a thorough rinse, and a trip through the dryer, then time to cool back into the temperature band, because penetrant goes onto a dry part within the band, not a hot wet one. Lighting checks come before processing. The ultraviolet lamps at the wash station and in the inspection booth should deliver at least one thousand microwatts per square centimeter at the working surface, with ambient white light in the booth held to about two footcandles, and your eyes given several minutes to adapt to the dark. Dip the rack in the penetrant tank, lift it, and let the parts drain on the rack for the dwell, film wet the whole time. Then the wash. Coarse water spray, modest pressure, no more than about forty pounds per square inch, water in the fifty to one hundred degree range, working under the ultraviolet lamp and watching the background fade. The instant the background is gone, stop. Now the diagnostic skill, because this is what separates a technician from a button pusher. Processing errors do not announce themselves when they happen. They show up later, disguised as inspection results, and you have to read them backwards. No indications on a part you had real reason to suspect. Think about what could seal the flaw or empty it. Metal smeared over the crack by wire brushing or blasting. A coating bridging the opening. Water or oil still sitting in the flaw. Penetrant that dried before it could work. Or a wash that stripped it out. Faint, washed-out indications point at the middle of the process. A dwell cut short. A film that went dry. An overwash. An emulsifier that sat too long. A heavy glowing background points at removal that did not finish, a rough surface, or contaminated rinse water. And before you blame the part or pass it, check the system. A known-defect comparator panel run through the same line tells you in one look whether the process is finding what it should. If the panel looks weak, stop inspecting and fix the process before you touch production hardware. Every error in this module is silent when it happens and loud at the end. The discipline that protects you is the same at every station. Verify before you process. Watch while it runs. Stop at the endpoint, not past it. And write down what you measured. Do that, and the back half of the process, developing, interpreting, and evaluating indications, has something real to work with. Practice these sequences until the order is automatic, because on the floor the order is the procedure, and the procedure is the inspection.
imagePrompt: A trainee and a supervisor in safety glasses move a small steel component along the stations of a penetrant processing line, from a cleaning bench past a dip tank toward a darkened wash booth glowing with UV light.

sections.heading: Run the Line Like a Checklist
sections.body:
Each step you have learned exists in a fixed order for a reason: every stage prepares the physics for the next one. Pre-clean and dry so flaws are **open and empty**. Verify lighting so processing and evaluation are **observable**. Apply penetrant and dwell so flaws **fill**. Remove the excess so the surface is **quiet** but the flaws stay **loaded**. Only then is the part ready for drying and developer - the back half of the process, covered in the next module.

The craft skill this lesson drills is **bidirectional**: running the sequence forward without skipping a verification, and reading inspection symptoms backward to the step that caused them. A processing error never complains at the moment it happens - a wire-brushed surface looks shiny, an overwashed part looks clean, a three-minute dwell looks efficient. The error only surfaces at evaluation, as a missing, faint, or buried indication. By then the only fix is to reprocess, so the cheap place to catch every error is at its own station, with a checklist habit.

sections.caption: The front half of the penetrant process with the checkpoint and the classic fault at each station, from pre-cleaning through removal.

sections.heading: Walkthrough 1: Valve Body in the Field - Visible Dye, Solvent Technique
sections.body:
A machined steel valve body needs a local examination of one face in the field. Type II visible penetrant, Method C removal.

- **Lighting first.** Meter the white light at the examination surface: commonly **≥ 100 fc (≈ 1,000 lux)**. A dim corner of a plant fails this before you open the kit.
- **Prepare.** Solvent-wipe the machine oil off the test area until a fresh cloth comes away clean. Allow **flash-off time** so solvent in any flaw evaporates - the surface drying first proves nothing about the flaw. Confirm the part is within the band, commonly **40-125 °F**.
- **Apply and dwell.** Spray an even red film over the area of interest **plus a surrounding band**. Start the timer: commonly **≥ 10 minutes**, film kept wet throughout.
- **Remove.** Dry lint-free wipe for the bulk; fresh solvent-**dampened** cloth for the traces; never flood the surface.

The part now waits, clean-surfaced and flaw-loaded, for nonaqueous developer. Every number above is a typical procedure value - the written procedure on the job governs.

sections.heading: Walkthrough 2: Casting Rack on a Fluorescent Water-Washable Line
sections.body:
A rack of aluminum castings runs a Type I, Method A line - the volume-production counterpart to the field job.

- **Prepare.** Alkaline clean to cut heavy soils, rinse thoroughly, and dry in the oven - part temperature commonly held **≤ 160 °F** - then cool back into the application band. Penetrant goes onto **dry, in-band** parts only.
- **Verify lighting before processing.** UV-A at the wash station and inspection booth: commonly **≥ 1,000 µW/cm²** at the working surface; booth ambient white **≤ 2 fc**; several minutes of dark adaptation before evaluating.
- **Apply and dwell.** Dip the rack, lift, and run the dwell as **drain dwell** - wet film on every surface, excess draining back to the tank, no pooling in pockets.
- **Remove.** Coarse water spray at an angle, commonly **≤ 40 psi** and **50-100 °F**, under the UV-A lamp, watching the background glow fade. **Stop the instant the background clears.**

Same physics as the field job, different scale - and the same two endpoints guarded: flaws full at the end of dwell, flaws still full at the end of the wash.

sections.title: Errors are silent at the station, loud at evaluation
sections.body: Nothing about a wire-brushed surface, a three-minute dwell, or an extra minute of spraying looks wrong **at the time** - each one quietly deletes indications that should have formed. You cannot inspect quality into the part at the end of the line, so the discipline is the same at every station: **verify before you process, watch while it runs, stop at the endpoint - and record what you measured.**

sections.heading: Reading Symptoms Backward
sections.body:
Diagnosis runs the process in reverse - from what evaluation shows to the station that caused it:

- **No indications where there is real reason to expect them.** Something sealed the flaw or emptied it: smeared metal from brushing or blasting, a coating bridging the opening, water or cleaner still in the flaw, penetrant that dried during dwell, or a stripping overwash.
- **Faint, broken, washed-out indications.** The flaw was only partly loaded or partly stripped: dwell cut short, cold part without extended dwell, film gone dry, wash run past background, or emulsifier contact run long.
- **Heavy background glow or dye haze.** Removal never finished: insufficient wash, surface too rough for the method, or contaminated rinse water re-depositing penetrant.

Before passing parts or condemning the process on a hunch, run the **known-defect comparator panel** through the same line. A strong panel says the process is healthy; a weak panel says stop inspecting production and fix the process first. That daily system performance check is the umpire for every symptom above.

sections.caption: Troubleshooting map from evaluation symptom back to the likely processing fault in preparation, application and dwell, or removal.
Station-by-station checklist with the classic fault and its effect - typical procedure values; the written procedure governs.
rows: Pre-clean & dry
Method matches contaminant; residues rinsed; flaws dry; part 40-125 °F
Wire brushing or blasting before PT; testing while flaws hold water
Sealed or blocked flaws - no indication
Lighting
≥ 100 fc white (Type II) or ≥ 1,000 µW/cm² UV-A with ≤ 2 fc ambient (Type I), at the surface
No meter check - 'looks bright enough'
Invalid examination; dim indications missed
Penetrant application
Continuous wet film, area of interest plus a band
Missed heat-affected zone or shadowed surface
Untested area passes by default
Dwell
Procedure minimum (commonly ≥ 10 min); film wet; extended when cold or flaws are tight
Dwell cut short; film dries unnoticed
Partially filled flaws - faint or missing indications
Removal
Method A: coarse spray ≤ 40 psi, 50-100 °F, stop at background. Method C: dry wipe then dampened wipe. B/D: timed emulsifier
Washing past background; flooding solvent; emulsifier overtime
Stripped flaws look clean; or background noise buries indications

interactive.title: Find the Processing Error
interactive.intro: Each card describes something that happened on a penetrant job. Classify the error by the stage of the process it corrupts.

items.label: Wire-brushed the weld bead to brighten it just before testing
items.why: Brushing smears ductile metal over crack mouths and seals them - a preparation fault that no later step can undo. An etch, or PT before brushing, is the fix.

items.label: Skipped the meter and judged the booth 'dark enough' by eye
items.why: Lighting is verified with calibrated meters - UV-A intensity at the surface and ambient white light are measured requirements, not impressions.

items.label: Tested the casting straight out of the rinse tank while still wet
items.why: Water sitting in flaws blocks or dilutes penetrant. Parts must be dried - and cooled into the band - before application.

items.label: Sprayed penetrant on the weld centerline only, missing the heat-affected zone
items.why: Coverage must include the area of interest plus a surrounding band. An unwetted heat-affected zone is an untested area that passes by default.

items.label: Started removal three minutes after the penetrant went on
items.why: Typical procedures require at least about ten minutes of dwell. Cutting it short leaves flaws partially filled and indications faint.

items.label: Let the film dry to a tacky skin during a long dwell without re-wetting
items.why: A dried film stops feeding the flaw and removes poorly. The rule during dwell is keep the film wet - re-apply when it dulls.

items.label: Rinsed with a needle-jet nozzle at 90 psi to speed things up
items.why: Method A removal uses a coarse droplet spray at commonly no more than about 40 psi. A high-pressure jet drives water into flaws and strips them - overwash.

items.label: Flooded the surface with solvent remover to finish the wipe faster
items.why: Free solvent runs into discontinuities and dissolves the trapped penetrant. Method C is a dry wipe, then a solvent-dampened cloth - never flooding.

items.label: Left parts in the lipophilic emulsifier for double the qualified contact time
items.why: Lipophilic emulsifier keeps diffusing into the penetrant the longer it sits - overtime contact reaches flaw-entrapped penetrant and rinses it away with the excess.

checkQuestions.question: On a fluorescent water-washable line, which sequence is correct for the casting rack?
options: Dip in penetrant → alkaline clean → wash → dwell
Alkaline clean → rinse → dry and cool into band → dip → drain dwell → coarse-spray wash under UV-A until background clears
Alkaline clean → dip while parts are still hot and wet → wash → dwell on the rack
Verify lighting after inspection is finished, then process the next rack
checkQuestions.explanation: Preparation (clean, rinse, dry, cool into band) precedes application; dwell runs as drain dwell after dipping; removal is a controlled coarse spray under UV-A that stops at background - and lighting is verified before processing.

checkQuestions.question: A part you had strong reason to suspect shows no indications at all, on a clean dark background. Which cause does that symptom pattern point AWAY from?
options: Metal smeared over the cracks before testing
An overwash that stripped the flaws
Insufficient final rinse leaving heavy residual background
Penetrant that dried before it could enter the flaw
checkQuestions.explanation: A clean dark background with missing indications points to sealed or emptied flaws (smear, dried penetrant, overwash). An insufficient rinse produces the opposite picture - visible background noise.

checkQuestions.question: Your white-light meter reads 60 fc at the surface for a visible PT evaluation that typically requires 100 fc. You should:
options: Proceed - 60 fc is more than half the requirement
Move the part closer to your eyes to compensate
Fix the lighting (add or reposition light), re-measure at the surface, and only then evaluate
Switch to fluorescent penetrant under the same lamp
checkQuestions.explanation: Lighting is a process requirement verified at the surface before evaluation. An examination below the procedure minimum is invalid - restore the light, measure, record, then inspect.

checkQuestions.question: The daily known-defect comparator panel comes through the line with noticeably weaker indications than yesterday. The correct response is to:
options: Inspect production parts anyway and re-run the panel tomorrow
Stop inspecting production, find and fix the process change, and re-verify with the panel before resuming
Increase developer time on production parts to compensate
Replace the panel, since panels wear out daily
checkQuestions.explanation: The panel is the system performance check: weak panel indications mean the process - wash, penetrant condition, emulsifier, lighting - has drifted. Production results from a sick process cannot be trusted.

checkQuestions.question: Halfway through a field job you realize you sprayed solvent remover directly onto the test surface during removal. The professional response is to:
options: Apply developer quickly before more penetrant dissolves
Wipe the surface dry and double the developer dwell
Note it on the report but continue, since visible dye is unaffected by solvent
Stop, clean the part, and reprocess from penetrant application - flooded solvent has likely dissolved flaw-entrapped penetrant
checkQuestions.explanation: Flooding solvent onto the surface is an overwash-class error: it reaches into flaws and dissolves the penetrant. Nothing downstream recovers it, so the part is cleaned and fully reprocessed.

references: CP-105-2024 PT Topical Outline §2.1-2.4 (liquid penetrant processing, applied practice)
ASTM E165 / ASTM E1417 (penetrant examination - general practice)
ASME BPVC Section V, Article 6 (liquid penetrant examination - general practice)
